prompt: Which work in the corpus contains a saint who addresses a marble pillar inside a heathen city, calling it 'better than gold' because the King of glory once inscribed the Ten Commandments on it and gave it to Moses (and 'as the truth-fast ones afterwards held it… Joshua and Tobias'), then commands it to spew a 'wide-running water for the ruin of men' upon the corrupt folk? Quote the saint's address to the stone and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The fusion of an animate marble pillar (figured as the original Sinai tablet kept by Joshua and Tobias) with a flood unleashed inside a city to drown its inhabitants is a signature, narratively unique passage of this work; no other Old English Christian poem in the corpus features the pillar-flood image, and the dharmaśāstra/Old Javanese/19th-century works are unrelated genres.

prompt: Which work in the corpus stages a dialogue at sea in which a saint, not yet recognizing that his helmsman is the disguised Lord of glory, praises him as a wave-rider who is 'young, not old in winters' yet steers a 'foam-necked' ship that fares 'most like a fowl' — and then says he himself has been 'sixteen times on a sea-boat' yet has never seen such a steersman? Quote the saint's praise of the helmsman and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: A specific numerical boast (sixteen prior sea-voyages) embedded in a Christ-as-disguised-helmsman dialogue is unique to this work. The Seafarer in the Old English Elegies has a sea-voyager but no disguised-Christ dialogue, no foam-necked-fowl simile, and no sixteen-voyages count; Elene and Juliana have no sea-journey at all.

prompt: Which work in the corpus narrates a saint breaking into a cannibal city's prison after seven gate-guards drop dead at his approach, embracing a fellow apostle inside, and then leading out a precisely numbered company of captives — 'one hundred and two… and also forty' men, plus 'of women there yet, in addition to the host, one less than fifty'? Quote the verses giving these numbers and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The exact triple-count of rescued prisoners (102 + 40 men, plus 49 women) following the seven dead doorwardens, inside an apostle-rescues-apostle scene, is unique to this work. Elene's True-Cross narrative, Juliana's martyrdom, and the anonymous Old English elegies contain no such jailbreak or count; the cannibal-city setting and Mermedonian frame are exclusive to Andreas.
